summary: A military reconnaissance mission report (MISREP) documents an ISR/SIGINT collection sortie supporting Operation Inherent Resolve, with an aircraft taking off from an airbase, conducting collection and support tasks over a specified grid location, then returning; the document is administrative aircraft mission reporting rather than an anomaly sighting report.
The document displays a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no other visible text or descriptive content, providing no information about a reported observation.
An imaging frame shows a small dark dot near a crosshair reticle overlaid on a grainy gray background, with no accompanying text describing the observation.
The document consists only of a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no accompanying body text or details about any observed object or incident.

This is a 1996 RTI technical report prepared for U.S. Air Force space wings describing statistical modeling of low-probability space-booster failure scenarios (Mode-5 failures) for launch risk calculations, including a history of Atlas, Delta, and Titan launch failures. It does not describe any anomalous aerial phenomenon.

This is an administrative front-matter set from the Vandenberg AFB Launch Summary (1958-2000), including a title page, distribution list, and foreword describing the document as an official registry of major launch operations from Vandenberg AFB compiled by the 30th Space Wing History Office; it does not describe any anomalous aerial observation.

An FBI Knoxville field office memo lists enclosures sent to the Bureau regarding reported 'flying saucers' seen over the Oak Ridge, Tennessee area during July 1947, including two photographs of the objects and a newspaper clipping from the Knoxville News-Sentinel.
This appears to be a document cover or index page referencing section 1.4(a), without visible body text describing an incident.

Email correspondence confirms two unclassified tearlines describing U.S. aircraft observations of a possible UAP on two dates, noting brief sightings at unknown altitude and speed with no interference reported.

locationRaw: Between Atjaty and Adzhijabul, Trans-Caucasus region, USSR
summary: A US Air Attache intelligence report describes three American observers traveling by rail in the Trans-Caucasus region of the USSR who reported seeing two round, disc-shaped aircraft ascend nearly vertically with rotating outer surfaces, then accelerate sharply northward to about 6000 feet; searchlight beams were seen near the reported takeoff area.

A U.S. Air Force mission report (MISREP) describes an ISR aircraft conducting operations over the eastern Mediterranean, during which one small unidentified aerial phenomenon was observed at 0117Z, alongside separate observations of reacting aircraft and a probable SU-27/35 landing near Al Assad airfield.

Apollo mission transcript records crew members observing bright, jagged, tumbling fragments or particles drifting past the spacecraft windows during a maneuver, possibly related to fluid, ice, or paint peeling from the S-IVB stage. The crew and ground control discuss the fragments' appearance, motion, and possible origin without reaching a definite conclusion.
A sensor or camera display with crosshair overlay and a timestamp shows a small dark point in the sky against a mountainous horizon; the accompanying document text is redacted.

A PAO release commentary transcript of Gemini 7/6 flight communications records astronaut Borman reporting a 'bogey' at ten o'clock high, followed by descriptions from Borman and Lovell of hundreds of small particles and a tumbling booster body observed near the spacecraft.

An aerial reconnaissance-style image shows crosshair targeting markers over a grayscale terrain view with a small dark circular object near the center. No accompanying text identifies the observer, date, or location of the depicted object.

A checklist-format field report describes a newspaper account from an editor in Norton, Kansas, of an object observed over Norcatur, Kansas that produced one big explosion followed by smaller explosions and left a bluish-white smoke smudge in a mushroom shape at high altitude before disintegrating.

This is an Apollo 17 crew debriefing on scientific experiments, including a discussion by a scientist named Henry about UV background radiation observations, X-ray and gamma-ray backgrounds, Lyman-alpha hydrogen radiation, and Earth spectrum observations made from the spacecraft. It contains no report of an anomalous aerial object.

An internal 1952 memorandum reports that an Air Force official told an inquirer that flying saucer phenomena remain a complete enigma, noting such reports have existed for over a century and that credible observers, including pilots and radar operators, are reporting incidents that cannot be fully explained by known causes. The memo states the Air Force does not seriously consider the objects to be man-made or foreign-controlled craft.

An aircrew report describes a round, cold object tracked in infrared over the Gulf of Aden for about 8 minutes, moving at 168 degrees and 277 mph at roughly 23,819 feet altitude, making a few abrupt directional changes; the object appeared as a bright white shape against a black-hot IR background.

This is a State Department political blotter cable summarizing Mexican political news for Sep 11-15, 2023, including one item noting the Mexican Congress heard testimony on alien life; the document itself provides no details of any specific sighting or incident.

A sensor or camera feed shows a small dark object with a faint companion blob near the frame center, with a timestamp visible but no accompanying descriptive text due to redaction. No details on observer, location, or context are available.
This appears to be a targeting or tracking reticle display with a small round object near the crosshair, rather than a narrative document; no descriptive text of an incident is visible.

An FBI FD-302 report describes a contractor conducting LiDAR/drone tests near a gated site who observed an elongated metallic/gray object with a bright banded white light on its east side, moving east to west at roughly 5000 feet, visible for five to ten seconds before the light extinguished and the object disappeared; a colleague was unable to see it despite attempts to point it out.

Apollo 17 crew debriefing describes an astronaut observing a fireball and light flashes during transearth flight, including a tunnel-like visual effect with a bright spot after the fireball dimmed, and continuous light flashes seen during dark adaptation periods; another crewmember also mentions mistaking an aircraft carrier's superstructure for an object during landing.

An FBI Los Angeles memo describes a magazine called Flying Saucers International, official journal of the Amalgamated Flying Saucer Clubs of America, whose July 1966 issue reportedly contains an article claimed to have been received via short-wave radio from an entity identified as Master Kalen-Li Retan of a planet called Korendor. The office found no derogatory information and took no further action.

A heavily redacted USCENTCOM MISREP describes an ISR aircraft mission during Operation Inherent Resolve, during which the crew reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon at a specified but redacted coordinate location during the mission.

A sensor or camera still with a timestamp shows two small dark spots near the center crosshair, with distance markers indicating a scale of about 3 units on each side; most surrounding text and identifying information is redacted.
An aerial reconnaissance-style image shows a small dark object near a targeting reticle over terrain, with no accompanying descriptive text visible on the page.

An FBI teletype reports that AEC security officials at the Savannah River Plant advised that two Du Pont Company employees saw a blue light with an orange fringe, shaped like a saucer, flying over the plant's Four Hundred Area on the evening of August 8, 1952, moving at high speed in a northeastern direction.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman
summary: A mission report (Misrep 4782130) from the 482ATKS documents an unmanned ISR aircraft supporting NAVCENT operations in the Arabian Gulf/Strait of Hormuz/Gulf of Oman area, during which the crew reported observing a UAP at 1732Z, with details referred to a separate observation line not included in this excerpt.

An FBI Detroit office memo reports that a caller, David Weaver, said he observed a circular object with a crystal-type dome that reflected lights, moving northward across Detroit near Six Mile and Lamphere Street early on the morning of April 17, 1958. Weaver stated he tried unsuccessfully to reach Selfridge Field before phoning the FBI office.

A December 1947 Air Materiel Command letter to the Chief of Staff, USAF, references prior reports on flying discs and states qualified observers continue to submit reports of the phenomenon, prompting continued intelligence collection and analysis. A follow-up December 1947 memo from Air Force headquarters directs AMC to establish a formal project (code named SIGN) to collect, evaluate, and distribute information on aerial sightings of potential national security interest.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman
summary: A U.S. Air Force mission report (Misrep 4592219) describes a reconnaissance aircraft flight over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman conducting SIGINT and IMINT collection in support of NAVCENT operations, noting that at one point the crew observed noteworthy activity documented separately in an observation log.

This FBI Detroit field office file contains a routing envelope and notation referencing notes from an interview with an individual named Wladyslaw Krasuski, received November 7, 1957. The pages provide no description of an observed object or event, only administrative and filing information.

The image shows a calibration or targeting display with a crosshair and tick-mark scale, with a timestamp overlay; large redacted blocks obscure most of the surrounding data and no descriptive content of an aerial phenomenon is visible.

A mission report from a 2-ship F-15E flight conducting defensive counter-air operations over Shaddadi describes two separate observations: three possible unidentified aerial phenomena at flight level 240 and, in a separate entry, a possible balloon at flight level 210. No further engagement or resolution is noted, and both events are marked as no factors to report.

The image shows a lunar surface photograph with several small bright anomalies in the sky area above the horizon, each enlarged in labeled inset boxes (Areas 1-5) showing bluish or whitish specks against dark background.

A military mission report describes an aircraft flying an ISR mission near Baghdad, Iraq, that observed a possible unidentified aerial phenomenon during its flight; further details are referenced in a separate UAP line entry but not included here.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman
summary: A 482ATKS mission report (Misrep 4685903) documents an ISR/AREC sortie flying support over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman, during which the crew received a guard call and reported observing one unknown formation during the mission.
Sensor or tracking display footage shows a small dark object near the crosshair marker with a timestamp of 12/31/99 18:19:06; no accompanying descriptive text is visible.

A memo from Hq. Eleventh Air Force forwards an FBI report describing a round, phosphorescent object seen at night by railroad workers near Hobson, Ohio, moving at great speed and high altitude with a phosphorescent trail.

locationRaw: Papua New Guinea (including Wewak and Angoram areas)
summary: A 1985 U.S. Embassy Port Moresby cable reports that Papua New Guinea's National Intelligence Organization inquired about high-altitude, high-speed aircraft reported over PNG on the evening of January 24, including radar contact by an Air Niugini pilot and multiple visual sightings of contrails and lights, with residents in Wewak reportedly frightened enough to prompt a public meeting attended by the Prime Minister.

A U.S. Air Force MISREP describes an aviator-reported incident during a tasked mission, but nearly all narrative details, including the object description, location, and personnel information, are redacted under exemption (b)(1)1.4a.

A heavily redacted field report describes a nighttime aerial search near a mountain range in 2025, during which a helicopter crew and observers on the ground reported multiple sightings of orb-like lights that flared up and down in formation, sometimes appearing near the aircraft and near unspecified military aircraft conducting a training mission. Witnesses described the objects as oval, orange with white/yellow centers, moving at high speed, and at times splitting into multiple objects before being lost from view.

Skylab 1/2 crew debriefing transcript in which astronauts Kerwin, Conrad, and Weitz discuss unusual visual phenomena such as light flashes, sunbursts, and streaks seen in their eyes, mostly while resting with eyes closed, and distinguish these from fire-sensor lights; a later section discusses medical kit usage.

A sensor/video still with an on-screen timestamp shows a small dark object near the center of the frame against a gray background, with a scale reference marked on the crosshair overlay; no accompanying narrative text is visible.

The image shows a targeting or scope-style reticle overlaid on a grainy grayscale field with a small dark dot near center, flanked by black redaction bars; no accompanying text describes the observation.

A U.S. Air Force mission report (124th Attack Squadron) describes an aircraft crew observing one unidentified aerial object during an ISR mission over a redacted area on 14 July 2024, with the sighting noted alongside routine target development activities.

A newspaper clipping reports that a Catholic priest in Grafton, Wisconsin found a round metal disc, about 18 inches in diameter and weighing four to five pounds, in his yard after hearing a whirring noise and an explosion, and turned it over to the FBI.

This appears to be a sensor or camera overlay frame with a targeting reticle and range markings, timestamped 12/31/99 18:11:19, but the surrounding descriptive text and imagery are redacted, leaving no discernible account of an observed object.
The document appears to be a targeting or reconnaissance grid overlay with reticle markings and a small marker point, but no descriptive text is visible to indicate what object or event was observed.

A USAF mission report (Misrep 9329374) describes an ISR aircraft on a support mission that observed one possible unidentified aerial phenomenon during on-station operations, with details of the sighting redacted or referenced only to a separate UAP attachment not included here.

A MISREP describes a 2-ship F-16CM flight out of Prince Sultan Air Base flying a defensive counter-air mission in the ESSA over Iraq and Syria. During the mission, at approximately 2302Z, the flight reportedly observed multiple possible unidentified aerial phenomena, referenced to a separate UAP report, with no further details in this narrative.

Apollo 12 mission transcripts document routine air-to-ground communications between Houston and the Intrepid/Yankee Clipper crews, including one crew member reporting particles of light seen through the LM's alignment optical telescope that appeared to originate near the observer and drift off into space, initially suspected to be water dropped from a boiler vent. A separate later exchange describes an LMP noting an anomalous flashing '8' pattern on the AGS DEDA display, later attributed by ground personnel to electromagnetic interference seen previously during spacecraft testing.

An FBI office memo summarizes Air Force-recorded observations since December 1948 of aerial phenomena near sensitive installations in New Mexico, classified as green fireballs, discs, and meteors. It notes about 150 such observations reported, an Air Force intelligence office's concern, and a scientific study contracted with a private firm to investigate.

locationRaw: between Everett and Bedford, near Boston, Massachusetts
summary: An Air Defense Command summary describes two Pan American Airways crew members, pilot Alpheus Powell and navigator Walter White, independently sighting unidentified elongated objects near Boston, Massachusetts while flying a Constellation aircraft from Gander to New York on 4 August 1947. Both described a bright orange/gold, cylindrical or elliptical object with blunt ends, viewed briefly at a distance before being lost from sight.

A lunar surface photograph showing astronaut shadows and a distant horizon, with an inset magnification highlighting a small vertical streak of blue light near the horizon that is not otherwise explained in the image.
An aerial reconnaissance-style image shows crosshair targeting markers over terrain with a dark circular mark near the reticle center; accompanying text is redacted.
A sensor or scope display with crosshair reticle and timestamp shows a small dark dot near center against a uniform gray background; no accompanying descriptive text is visible due to redaction.

A gun/targeting camera style still with a crosshair reticle shows a small dark helicopter-like object against a gray sky, with a timestamp reading 12/31/99 18:10:02. Multiple areas of text are redacted with black bars.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman
summary: A military mission report (Misrep 5039166) describes an aircraft supporting a NAVCENT operation over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman, during which the crew observed a UAP twice while conducting reconnaissance tasking.
A gunsight or targeting camera frame shows crosshairs over a plain gray background with a small dark dot near center; no contextual data or narrative accompanies the image.

Federal law enforcement agents in the western U.S. reported multiple sightings, including orange orbs launching smaller red orbs, a large glowing orange orb hovering near a rock formation, and a dark kite-shaped object with red and white lights that moved laterally off a road at 15-20mph.
The document consists solely of a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no accompanying descriptive text or incident details visible on the page.

A sensor or camera reticle image shows a small dark point object against a plain sky background, with a timestamp and range markings but no accompanying descriptive text visible due to redaction.

A military mission report describes a flight crew detecting one unidentified aerial phenomenon during return-to-base near the end of an ISR sortie; extensive details of the mission and personnel are redacted, and no further description of the object is given.

An FBI report describes New Mexico State Police Officer Lonnie Zamora reporting an unidentified object that had landed and then departed southwest of Socorro; investigators later found four rectangular ground depressions, burned grass patches, and three smooth circular marks at the site, with no other persons or objects observed nearby.

A sensor or camera overlay display with a reticle and range markings shows a small dark spot near the center crosshair, with surrounding data fields redacted; no descriptive text is visible to explain the observation.

A radar or sensor display frame shows a small dark blob near the crosshair center, with a timestamp overlay; four data blocks along the edges have been redacted, leaving no readable identifying details.

This appears to be a sensor or camera calibration frame with a crosshair reticle and range markings, timestamped 12/31/99 18:12:20, with no visible object or narrative content; multiple sections of the frame are redacted.

An email exchange discusses approval to include the year in an unclassified tear line describing a sighting by U.S. aircraft of a possible unidentified anomalous phenomenon on October 31; the object was oval/orb shaped, moving at low speed, and was observed for over two hours.

A range fouler debrief form describes an ISR mission in which a solid white round object flew through the sensor's field of view at night, appearing to make erratic movements above the water; the crew intermittently tracked it, achieving 4x zoom before losing it due to poor track placement.

A Navy squadron pilot reported observing three unidentified small aerial contacts at night during routine operations in the North Arabian Sea, with negative radar, ES, and IFF tracking. The contacts were round, appeared to have wings/airframe features, maintained relative course, speed, and altitude, and were seen moving westward with no interaction with the aircraft.
A sensor or radar-derived image displays a small dark object marked with a 3-unit measurement scale, with no accompanying witness or location narrative visible.
An annotated lunar surface photograph shows an astronaut's shadow on the ground with a magnified inset highlighting a small bright point of light in the dark sky above the horizon.
A sensor or camera frame with a timestamp shows two small dark dots near the center crosshair, with no accompanying text describing the observation available due to redactions.

A 2001 State Department cable from Moscow relays a Russian MFA official's dismissal of reports of unidentified planes over Georgia's Kodori Gorge as comparable to 'UFO' sightings, in the context of denying Russian military bombing runs there.

An instrumentation frame with a reticle overlay shows a small dark spot or pair of dark marks near the center crosshair, with a timestamp overlay but no accompanying descriptive text visible in the image.

locationRaw: Kansas City, Kansas and Olathe, Kansas
summary: A Lowry Flight Service Center report describes two spherical objects seen over Kansas City, Kansas and Olathe, Kansas on the night of January 6, 1950, by two Bendix Aviation employees, one a pilot. The objects appeared brilliant white with orange and red flashes, remained motionless for ten to fifteen minutes at an estimated altitude of seven to eight thousand feet, then departed rapidly to the southwest with no sound or exhaust trail observed.

A 1950 letter from a self-described Mexican inventor to a scientific commission president discusses stratospheric aerostats, popularly called flying saucers, describing them as apparatuses combining global and parachute stability that could be launched from the ground or air.
A sensor or camera still shows a small dark object near the center of a crosshair reticle, with a timestamp overlay but no accompanying textual description of the sighting.

locationRaw: in transit between Earth and the moon
summary: Apollo 11 crew debriefing transcript in which Aldrin describes sighting an unidentified object approximately one day out from the moon, which the crew examined with a monocular and initially considered might be the S-IVB stage, though ground control reported the S-IVB was 6000 miles away. Armstrong compared the object's appearance to an open suitcase, and Aldrin noted it seemed sizeable and within their vicinity.
This appears to be a sensor or camera calibration frame with a crosshair reticle and timestamp overlay, containing no discernible object or narrative content describing an incident.

An FBI FD-302 report describes a base contractor supervisor and a passenger who observed a large cigar-shaped, metallic bronze object with a bright diamond-white ringed light on one end, hovering and slowly moving east to west above a tree line before disappearing after five to ten seconds. The witness noted no sound, no contrails, and no engine interference, and had never seen anything like it in fifteen years working with military aircraft and drones.
An annotated lunar surface photograph highlights two small bright specks in the dark sky above the horizon, labeled Area 1 and Area 2, with enlarged crops shown for closer inspection.

An FBI office memo from the Cincinnati field office to the Director forwards additional information gathered from a witness named Thomas Eickhoff regarding a subject referencing Truman Bethurum and flying discs, filed under an espionage-related miscellaneous investigation heading.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman
summary: A misrep documents a military ISR aircraft mission over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman that included multiple guard calls and one observation of a UAP during the flight, with limited further detail on the object's characteristics provided in the visible text.
The document displays only a section or exhibit label, '1.4(a)', with no descriptive narrative or incident details present on the page.

An FBI form letter from Director Hoover responds to Mrs. Levi J. Dow of Goffstown, New Hampshire, stating the Bureau found no record of her correspondent or the referenced organization and does not evaluate the integrity of organizations or individuals. The letter does not describe any specific sighting or object.
An artistic rendering depicts a metallic disc-shaped object with a bright light emanating from its underside, hovering over a grassy field near a tree line.

locationRaw: Keithsburg, Iowa and Ascension Parish, Louisiana
summary: Two incident summary sheets describe separate sightings: one near Keithsburg, Iowa in September 1948 of a metallic, aluminum-colored disc-shaped object about 12 feet in diameter drifting south at high altitude, and another in Ascension Parish, Louisiana in October 1948 of a white-hot, cone-shaped object encased in flame flying east at about 200 mph with no sound or trail.

A U.S. Air Force mission report describes an armed reconnaissance and SIGINT/IMINT flight over Iraq/Syria on 30-31 July 2022, during which the aircrew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon at a specified location during the mission; the report provides minimal descriptive detail of the object itself.

A U.S. Air Force ISR mission report (Misrep 9337873) from a 33 SOS/27 SOW aircraft describes the crew conducting FMV/SIGINT collection on-station when they observed one possible unidentified aerial phenomenon, referenced in an attached exhibit (UAP 1) that is not included here.

An FBI file cover and record for case 62-83894 serial 403, along with a book jacket for Gray Barker's 'They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers,' which describes claims that saucer researchers who challenged government explanations were visited and silenced by unidentified men.

A folder and correspondence introduce Carol Rosin and Jon Cypher requesting a meeting to discuss aerospace policy matters, and a handwritten note references the French Cometa report along with witness testimony about radar and air traffic control observations near Mexico City International Airport.

An August 1947 FBI San Francisco field office memo to headquarters forwards Army Air Forces reports of additional flying disc sightings by persons in Oregon and Arizona, along with results of the military's investigations conducted before the Bureau took over such inquiries.

A sensor display frame with a crosshair reticle shows a small dark spot near the center against a grainy gray background; associated identifying text along the top and sides has been redacted, leaving only a timestamp visible.

An FBI office memo and an accompanying Army Air Forces letter discuss a report by F.M. Johnson of Portland, Oregon, who claimed to have sighted a flying disc on June 24, 1947. Fourth Air Force intelligence forwarded the report to the FBI, noting similarities to Kenneth Arnold's earlier sighting and requesting that Johnson be interviewed rather than investigated by the Air Force.

A calibration or sensor test frame shows a crosshair reticle with a small dark point near center, accompanied by heavy black redaction bars and a large blacked-out header; no descriptive narrative of an observed object is visible.

A U.S. Air Force MISREP describes an aircrew conducting an ISR mission that observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon during flight operations, with further FMV and SIGINT collection continuing afterward.

locationRaw: Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman
summary: A military mission report (MISREP) describes an aircrew operating over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman region that reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon on three separate occasions during a single sortie.

An FBI Dallas field office memo to the Director references a Kansas City letter concerning flying discs and states the matter is being referred to OSI without further investigation, with no other action taken per existing instructions.

A billeting officer at Muroc Air Field, California reported seeing two silver disc-like or spherical objects flying about 300 MPH at roughly 8000 feet, later observing a third similar object performing a tight circle over the airfield; several other witnesses corroborated the sighting.

An Air Force debrief form describes an aircrew's radar and targeting-pod contact with two unknown 'range fouler' objects during a night defensive counter-air mission over an unspecified working area. The pod showed two infrared-significant contacts, one circling the other, exhibiting red blinking strobes and noise jamming before disappearing rapidly; the crew could not close within 16.9 nautical miles for a better identification.

An FBI FD-302 report describes a witness driving to a LiDAR test site with several colleagues who observed a bright white stationary light over the horizon that then moved to the right and disappeared after about ten seconds; the witness estimated it was ten to twenty miles away and speculated it might have been a meteor.

An FBI Portland memo describes Portland police radio logs and two officer accounts of an unidentified bright silvery object seen flying over the city on September 11, 1947, moving from east to south at estimated speeds and altitudes with no oscillation, believed by one officer to possibly be sunlight reflecting off a conventional airplane.
The page shows only a section heading, '1.4(a)', with no additional visible text or content describing an incident.

A range fouler reporting form from the 172 ATKS squadron describes a sensor operator tracking a round, cold object in infrared over the Gulf of Aden on 15 October 2020, appearing bright white against a black-hot background while making a few abrupt directional changes over about a minute.
A gunsight or targeting camera video still shows a small dark aerial object near a reticle overlay, with a timestamp of 12/31/99 18:10:00; multiple areas of the frame are redacted.

A lunar surface photograph shows an astronaut's shadow with a bright point of light visible in the sky above the horizon, highlighted and magnified via an inset in the image; no accompanying text explains the source of the point of light.

A 1963 NASA Space Council memo to the State Department discusses policy considerations should intelligent life be discovered in space, weighing scientific consensus against flying saucer advocates' claims and speculating about possible extraterrestrial activity on Mars or the Moon.
A lunar surface photograph shows a rocky horizon and foreground shadow with a highlighted inset zooming in on two small light-colored points visible in the dark sky above the terrain.

An Air Force pilot's debrief form reports that during a dusk ISR mission on 08/31/20, an object was tracked on sensor after being seen flying through the screen; it was surpassed by a second similar object of higher speed, and at one point three such objects appeared moving among each other. The objects were described as round with apparent propulsion and were assessed as moving at approximately 150 degrees/230 knots.

SHAEF Air Staff correspondence from March 1945 discusses reports of night phenomena described as balls of fire ('foo fighters') observed by aircrews, including Bomber Command crews reporting similar sightings for some time; the Air Ministry suggests some sightings may have been Me.262 jets and the rest possibly flak rockets, but states no definite explanation could be given. A related memo notes that photographs of 'long cylindrical objects' claimed by a 107th P.R. Squadron pilot were unsuccessful.

A declassified overhead surveillance image, cleared for public release, shows a faint bright streak or point of light circled in red over a terrain background; no accompanying narrative describes the observation circumstances.

Infrared sensor footage from a U.S. military platform reportedly shows an area of contrast entering the frame's bottom left, moving in a generally linear path toward the top of the screen, and exiting the top left area of the field of view.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly captured an area of contrast resembling an eight-pointed star that moved within the sensor's field of view, leaving a visible trail before exiting and re-entering the frame across the recording.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly captured five seconds of footage showing a faint area of contrast moving across the corner of the frame, entering from the right edge and exiting near the bottom center of the screen. No oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporting unit.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded a small thermal signature that brightened against the background, with the sensor panning and adjusting contrast and zoom to track it over about a minute.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded an area of contrast entering the frame and being tracked as the sensor panned from left to right for about 90 seconds, according to a report submitted to AARO by U.S. Central Command in 2020. No oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporter.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform recorded an area of contrast that entered the frame, intermittently faded and reappeared, and was tracked by the sensor as it panned and switched imaging modes over nearly five minutes in 2020. No oral or written witness description accompanied the footage.

Infrared sensor footage from a U.S. military platform reportedly shows an area of contrast being tracked against the background for several minutes before it exits the frame; no oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporting command.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform tracked and locked onto an area of contrast that grew more distinct and larger over the course of roughly a minute before it moved out of frame and exited the sensor's field of view.

Infrared footage from a sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly shows an area of contrast resembling a football-shaped body with three radial projections, one vertical and two angled downward, over nine seconds in 2024.
An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded three areas of contrast that maintained fixed positions and orientation relative to one another for nearly two minutes.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in the Indo-Pacific region reportedly tracked an area of contrast that remained near the center of the frame for the duration of the recording. No oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporting command.

Infrared video from a U.S. military platform reportedly shows a sensor tracking two areas of contrast, adjusting zoom and centering while following the objects over the course of about a minute and 49 seconds. No oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporting service member.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded two seconds of footage showing a small area of contrast moving across the field of view before exiting near the bottom right of the frame. No oral or written description of the observation was provided by the reporting command.

A U.S. Air Force MISREP describes an armed overwatch mission over Iraq/Syria (38SKC grid) during which the aircrew reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon while conducting a precision-guided munition shot; the mission otherwise proceeded normally with weapons calibration and multiple munitions released.

A 2004 State Department cable from Embassy Ashgabat describes a Turkmen NGO called the Union of UFOlogists, originally formed to study life on other planets, which had shifted to practical civic work such as business registration and humanitarian aid distribution. The document reports no confirmed UFO sightings in Turkmenistan, noting officials had occasionally consulted the group's president about unexplained occurrences in Turkmen airspace.

A State Department cable reports that Tajik Air's chief pilot and two American crew members saw a bright, high-speed light while flying at 41,000 feet over Kazakhstan, which maneuvered in circles and sharp turns for about forty minutes before departing at high speed; the crew later flew beneath high-altitude contrails they attributed to the object's path.

A mission report from the 50th ATKS describes a remotely piloted aircraft conducting ISR under Operation Spartan Shield that observed two unidentified aerial phenomena during a single sortie on separate occasions, with details referred to separate UAP report lines.

locationRaw: between Newark, New Jersey and Detroit, Michigan (over Philipsburg and Bradford, Pennsylvania/Cleveland, Ohio)
summary: FBI file materials compile press clippings about UFO sightings, including a pizza shop owner's photo of a saucer-like object in Grand Blanc, Michigan and a pilot's report of three bright objects trailing an airliner near Detroit.

An FBI San Antonio memo reports that Fourth Army G-2 relayed a newspaper article stating a business club in Alexandria, Louisiana was planning a convention for people who had seen flying saucers, noting that flying disks were reported twice in the area the week before the article ran.

Correspondence between AFOSI officials regarding a request to declassify or process an IIR (intelligence information report) summary. The emails discuss classification review procedures rather than describing an actual sighting or object.

A sensor image with measurement markings shows a small dark object near the center crosshair, with a scale reference of 3 units on each side. Most surrounding text and identifying information has been redacted.

A U.S. Navy P-8A patrol aircraft monitoring activity in the Eastern Mediterranean observed an unidentified low-flying object about 55 nautical miles northwest of Latakia, Syria, traveling southeast at approximately 500 knots for about two minutes; sensor data suggested a possible missile-like track passing near a Russian vessel and another unidentified vessel.

A U.S. Air Force mission report from an Operation Inherent Resolve sortie states that during ISR/SIGINT collection an aircrew observed one unidentified aerial phenomenon at 1514Z, with details referenced in an accompanying observation log; the narrative provides no further description of the object's appearance or behavior.

This is an Air Force policy memorandum (Air Intelligence Requirements Memorandum Number 4) establishing procedures for reporting sightings of unconventional aircraft and unidentified flying objects, including so-called flying discs, rather than a report of a specific incident.

A November 1948 U.S. Air Force intelligence memo transmits extracts from a teletype forwarding a complete set of reports prepared by a European Command special intelligence organization, sent for inspection and possible use in arguing for Air Force funding; no specific sighting or aerial object is described in this cover material.

A military mission report describes an aircraft crew conducting FMV/SIGINT collection sorties during Operation INHERENT RESOLVE; during one sortie the crew reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon, with details referenced to a separate UAP annex not included here.

A handwritten letter to Mr. Tuck recalls green light sightings near Los Alamos between 1948 and 1951, typically observed in the evening in the Jemez Mountains, and one afternoon sighting of five objects flying in formation from southeast to northwest, all reportedly logged by the Protective Force.
A Pantex facility incident report includes ground surveillance radar tower imagery and Sandia National Labs enhanced images of an unidentified object, with most descriptive text redacted.

A club newsletter from the Pajarito Astronomers announces an upcoming meeting where a scientist affiliated with a national laboratory group will speak on why scientists should be concerned about UFOs; the document itself does not describe any specific sighting.

A senior U.S. intelligence officer aboard a helicopter, along with a colleague, pilots, and a ground team, reported observing multiple orange oval-shaped orbs with white or yellow centers over a mountainous test range at night. The orbs reportedly approached within ten feet of the aircraft, split apart, formed patterns including a T-shape and triangle, and matched the speed and path of fighter jets before disappearing.

locationRaw: Sary Shagan testing range, Site 7, USSR
summary: A field report from a former Soviet citizen who worked at the Sary Shagan weapons testing range describes an evening in late summer 1973 when he stepped outside during a television broadcast and observed a bright green circular object or mass in the sky west of Site 7, which widened into concentric green circles over 10-15 seconds before the coloring disappeared within minutes; no sound was noted and the source offered no explanation.

locationRaw: over water within the United States Central Command area of responsibility, near Iran
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the CENTCOM area reportedly recorded four areas of contrast moving across the frame over water near Iran, followed by an additional area of contrast entering the frame moments later.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded an area of contrast that the sensor tracked before it rapidly exited the frame, an event assessed as likely occurring within the U.S. Central Command area of responsibility in 2021.

Infrared footage reportedly from a U.S. military sensor platform shows four areas of contrast, described as forming a formation, moving across the frame and being tracked with varying zoom and filtering over roughly eight minutes before the areas become indistinct as video quality degrades.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility reportedly recorded an area of contrast transiting the field of view from left to right in October 2022.

locationRaw: United States European Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly recorded an area of contrast in the frame that the sensor operator tracked as it moved, with the footage noted to have been digitally altered before it reached a classified network in a way that affected its apparent movement characteristics.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared sensor footage reportedly from a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area shows one or more areas of contrast entering, exiting, and being tracked across the frame over roughly five minutes.

Infrared footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military platform operating in Afghanistan shows a spherical object moving in and out of clouds, appearing as an area of contrast that transits across the frame before losing distinctiveness against the background; the video was later processed with zoom, sharpening, and slowed playback for review.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility reportedly recorded an area of contrast entering and being tracked in the frame, with the sensor operator zooming and panning to follow it over several minutes.

Infrared footage reportedly recorded from a U.S. military platform in the Persian Gulf region in August 2020 shows an area of contrast that transits across the frame and is tracked briefly by the sensor before becoming indistinguishable from video grain.

An infrared video reportedly from a U.S. military platform shows an area of contrast, described in the uploaded title as a spherical object, appearing to pulse over water while remaining generally centered in frame as sensor contrast modes shift.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared footage reportedly captured by a sensor aboard a U.S. military platform in the Central Command area of responsibility shows an area of contrast being tracked, panned, and zoomed in on before repeatedly exiting and re-entering the frame.

Infrared sensor footage reportedly captured aboard a U.S. military platform over the Yellow Sea shows areas of contrast entering and moving through the frame while the sensor pans, zooms, and tracks the phenomena; the uploader-assigned title describes it as spherical in appearance.

Infrared footage reportedly from a U.S. military platform's sensor shows the sensor tracking an area of contrast and several areas of contrast entering and exiting the field of view over the Gulf of Arabia region.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An electro-optical/infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area reportedly recorded an area of contrast entering and moving across the frame, which the sensor operator tracked, zoomed on, and re-acquired after a brief modality change.

An infrared video reportedly recorded by a sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Indo-Pacific region shows an area of contrast tracked near the center of the frame, with the sensor cycling contrast modes and panning to keep the object in view over roughly ten minutes.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Gulf of Arabia region reportedly tracked what appeared, at higher magnification, as multiple distinct areas of contrast that moved in and out of the sensor's field of view before exiting the frame.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the U.S. Central Command area reportedly recorded multiple areas of contrast entering and moving across the frame in 2018, with the sensor panning and zooming to track them as they separated and additional areas of contrast later appeared.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area reportedly recorded an area of contrast that was tracked, zoomed, and reacquired by the sensor operator over roughly five minutes in 2020.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the CENTCOM area of responsibility reportedly recorded an area of contrast entering its field of view on September 25, 2019, which the sensor operator tracked and magnified through several contrast and zoom adjustments before the recording ends.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared and full-motion video footage reportedly from a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area in 2021 shows an area of contrast crossing the sensor's field of view, with the sensor panning and zooming to track it before it re-enters frame and is tracked again.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared and full-motion video footage from a U.S. military sensor platform in the U.S. Central Command area reportedly shows an area of contrast entering the frame, being tracked by the sensor, and then becoming indistinct against the background as the sensor changed modes.

Infrared footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military sensor over the Persian Gulf area shows multiple areas of contrast that the sensor repeatedly tracks, zooms in and out on, and loses against the background before regaining.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility reportedly recorded multiple areas of contrast entering and exiting the frame in 2019, with the sensor panning to track the objects at various points.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared and full-motion video footage reportedly recorded by a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility shows a sensor panning and adjusting zoom to keep an area of contrast, described as a spherical object, centered in frame.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Full-motion and infrared sensor footage reportedly captured aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the U.S. Central Command area shows a sensor tracking an area of contrast near the center of the field of view before panning away.

An infrared video reportedly captured by a U.S. military sensor over the Kabul area shows an area of contrast entering the frame from the left and moving out of view to the right within about one second.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. Coast Guard platform reportedly recorded an area of contrast that it tracked by panning for roughly 32 seconds before the sensor panned away and the object left the frame.

Infrared footage reportedly from a U.S. Coast Guard sensor operating over the southeastern United States shows an area of contrast crossing the frame twice; in both instances the sensor failed to track it before it left the field of view.

Infrared footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military sensor near a submarine on March 25, 2022, shows multiple areas of contrast entering and exiting the frame, some appearing to move in and out of the water, while the sensor operator pans to track them.

locationRaw: United States Northern Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared footage reportedly captured by a fifth-generation military aircraft's sensor on January 20, 2023 shows an area of contrast tracked near the center of the frame, which later moves toward the edge of the field-of-view before a second area of contrast becomes visible.

locationRaw: United States Northern Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared sensor footage reportedly from a U.S. military aircraft shows a sensor tracking an area of contrast, with a reticle briefly locking onto it before losing track.

locationRaw: Eglin AFB, United States Northern Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly tracked an area of contrast by panning to keep it centered and cycling through contrast modes; the area reportedly lost distinctiveness against the background about 22 seconds into the recording.

Infrared sensor footage reportedly from a U.S. military aircraft shows an area of contrast tracked in the frame, followed by what the uploader described as a kinetic interaction in which the object appeared to fragment in a radial pattern.

A video reportedly from a commercial cellular device's rear camera shows a bright phenomenon with trails of diminishing brightness extending from its center, with the camera panning and zooming on it near Karaganda International Airport, Kazakhstan.

An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating near Columbus, Ohio reportedly recorded two areas of contrast that moved through the sensor's field of view, with one briefly obscured by a redacted display element before the sensor lost track of both.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military sensor in the U.S. Central Command area in June 2022 shows the sensor repeatedly tracking, zooming on, and panning to follow one or more areas of contrast that move through and exit the frame at various points.
An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly panned to track an area of contrast over the East China Sea before losing sight of the object at the 18-second mark.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Footage reportedly captured by a full-motion video camera aboard a U.S. military platform in the U.S. Central Command area of responsibility shows an area of contrast entering the frame, being tracked and zoomed in on by the sensor as it stays near the center, before it exits the lower left of the screen.

locationRaw: East Coast, United States (NORTHCOM area of responsibility)
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly tracked an area of contrast off the East Coast in December 2019, with the sensor changing contrast modes while following the object before losing track of it.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility shows an area of contrast crossing the frame from bottom to top on three brief occasions.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared sensor footage reportedly captured by a U.S. military platform in the Central Command area of responsibility shows multiple areas of contrast entering, transiting, and exiting the frame while the sensor cycles zoom levels and tracks one of the objects.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area reportedly recorded multiple brief instances of an area of contrast entering, crossing, and exiting the camera's field of view, with the sensor at times panning toward or attempting to track it.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the CENTCOM area of responsibility reportedly recorded an area of contrast that the sensor tracked and zoomed in on before it exited the frame.

locationRaw: United States Africa Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared sensor footage reportedly from a U.S. military platform operating in the U.S. Africa Command area of responsibility shows an area of contrast crossing the field of view for a few seconds, entering near the top left and exiting near the bottom right of the frame.

locationRaw: United States Africa Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared video reportedly captured by a sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the U.S. Africa Command area of responsibility shows a brief area of contrast crossing the frame from mid-left to upper-right around the 28-second mark.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area reportedly tracked an area of contrast entering and moving within the frame, cycling zoom and sensor modalities before the contrast faded and briefly reappeared.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform reportedly tracked a low-contrast area whose intensity increased over time, then panned to track a second area of contrast that transited the frame, within the U.S. Central Command area of responsibility in November 2020.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Full-motion video reportedly captured by a camera aboard a U.S. military platform shows a brief area of contrast entering the frame from the lower right and exiting the upper left over roughly two seconds.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: Infrared sensor footage from a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area of responsibility reportedly shows a low area of contrast tracked by the sensor, with a second area of contrast appearing and briefly tracked as well, over roughly five minutes in November 2020.

locationRaw: United States Central Command area of responsibility
summary: An infrared sensor aboard a U.S. military platform operating in the Central Command area reportedly tracked multiple areas of contrast, described as up to three fast-moving objects, over a four-minute period in October 2020.

This 1949 secret memo from Headquarters, Detachment D, 1100th USAF Special Reporting Group at Camp Campbell, Kentucky, is a follow-up security inspection report addressing gate locks, alert plans, radio communications, and erosion control measures; it does not describe any aerial observation. A separate cover sheet from Sandia Base's classified documents library lists the file subject as unidentified flying objects for 1949, but no incident details are given.

An FBI cover sheet references an enclosure of seven photostats depicting flying saucers, received from Department of the Air Force Headquarters on August 27, 1952, along with routine indices search and FOIPA processing slips.
A rendered night scene depicts a group of small red lights alongside a larger glowing orange orb positioned over a mountain ridge, with no accompanying textual documentation of an actual observation.

An Air Force UFO checklist describes a 1948 fireball explosion observed over Norcatur, Kansas, accompanied by a loud blast and smaller explosions, with a bluish-white smoke trail left in the sky. Follow-up notes state meteorite fragments, including a 109-pound stony achondrite, were later recovered, and an amateur photograph captured the resulting vapor trail.
An image shows two silhouetted figures observing a night sky containing a line of small red lights and one larger pale light above a mountainous horizon.
An image depicts a rural roadway at dusk with a bright orange orb-like object and smaller red points hovering above distant hills; no accompanying text establishes date, location, or witness details.

A CIA information report from a naturalized U.S. citizen of Hungarian descent relays a November 1955 letter from his niece in Budapest describing fast-moving flying objects, popularly called flying saucers, that reportedly kept scientific groups busy and were estimated to travel at 12,000 km per hour. The letter included a sketch showing several circles in a line formation running roughly from Budapest toward Moscow.

An FBI FD-302 documents an interview with an Army soldier at Fort Carson who reported that in early February 2022, he and four others observed a motionless, matte white, bean-shaped object hovering over a saddle in Cheyenne Mountain for several minutes before it vanished. The witnesses later compared independently drawn sketches that matched, and one reportedly filed an official report and was later contacted by AARO in 2024.

An FBI electronic communication documents contact with a witness who reported repeated observations, since November 2021, of unidentified anomalous phenomena appearing as plasma orbs near their residence, sometimes hovering statically or moving near trees and water. The witness provided video clips and reported correlating spikes in gamma radiation and unusual effects on electronics and GPS devices during observations.

An illustrative government graphic depicts four separate incidents reported by U.S. federal law enforcement agents in the western United States over several days in October 2023, describing orb-like objects (including one described as launching smaller orbs and another as a fiery orb near a ridgeline) as well as thin, kite-shaped objects with colored lights observed at close range.

A federal law enforcement agent's first-hand narrative describes multiple sightings over two days in October 2023 in the western United States, including a bright orb-like light that expelled smaller red lights, hovering red light formations, and orb-like lights resembling headlights that moved erratically and accelerated instantly across rugged terrain while agents attempted to approach them.

This is a preliminary NASA transcript of the Gemini IV flight crew debriefing, conducted aboard the USS Wasp and concluded at the Manned Spacecraft Center in June 1965, covering operational aspects of the mission rather than a specific anomalous sighting.

Two administrative tracking records log incoming congressional correspondence forwarded to a legal/policy office: one from Senator Snowe relaying a constituent letter about UFOs and recent Mars photographs, and another from Senator Grassley relaying a constituent request for information on reported UFO sightings by astronauts. Neither record describes a specific sighting or object.

A draft transcript describes an astronaut's debriefing account of attempting to use an eye patch to become dark/night adapted during a spaceflight, noting difficulties with the patch sticking and discussing observed star counts and dark adaptation timing during orbit, with no anomalous object reported.

This document is the cover and title pages of a CIA historical monograph on overhead reconnaissance programs, covering the U-2 and OXCART aircraft programs from 1954 to 1974. It is an administrative history publication, not a report of an observed aerial phenomenon.

A 1967 CIA information report recounts a US astrophysicist's conversations with Soviet astronomers during a research trip through the USSR, describing their varying levels of interest in and knowledge of unidentified flying object sightings, including a reddish flashing object seen by astronomers in Kiev and a 'peculiar object' observed near Crimea.

An AARO memorandum records a federal law enforcement agent's first-hand account of two sightings in October 2023 in the western United States, describing an orange orb that expelled smaller red orbs, and a second night with red lights hovering over an airfield and a vehicle that seemed to evade approach before disappearing.

An internal memo dated January 1958 discusses follow-up contact with a Dr. Leon Davidson regarding a closed case involving a 'space message' and its transmitter; the writer notes records on the matter were reportedly destroyed and describes prior evasive answers given to Davidson about the agency's involvement in his contacts with two named individuals.

A 1972 CIA intelligence report describes a Soviet conference on the origins of life in Yerevan, Armenia in September 1971, where scientists Kardashev and Sakharov presented a speculative physics paper on charged mass collapse and black holes, and another Soviet speaker gave a talk on a USSR committee investigating unusual aerial phenomena (flying saucers), which colleagues reportedly treated with humor and skepticism.

A CIA information report describes an unidentified object hovering at high altitude over Harare International Airport, Zimbabwe, on the afternoon of 2 July 2008, reportedly observed by radar and visually. Witnesses described a disc-like object with a hollow center and rotating lights that changed color before it rapidly ascended out of visual range, prompting a facility to be placed on high alert.

A 1953 Secretary of Defense routing slip transmits a CIA cover letter forwarding a scientific panel's report on unidentified flying objects (referred to colloquially as flying saucers) for review and action.

A CIA Domestic Collection Division report on Soviet Aeroflot fatigue-countermeasure research also notes that Dr. Akoyev told colleagues that 99 percent of UFO-type occurrences were traceable to natural or man-made phenomena, with about one percent unexplained and possibly attributable to hallucination.

Newspaper clippings compiled by the FBI describe multiple witnesses in Pasco and Kennewick, Washington reporting a fast-moving round object with flashing lights, described variously as a 'giant pinwheel' saucer and a disc with lights around its perimeter; some accounts noted it appeared round at times and triangular at times.

locationRaw: western United States, near a sensitive national security site
summary: An AARO memo describes six federal law enforcement agents who reported observing orange 'mother orbs' near a sensitive national security site in the western U.S. over two days in October 2023, with the orbs appearing to launch clusters of smaller red orbs that moved, hovered, and in one case remained stationary for hours; the phenomena were described as silent and remain unresolved after analysis of flight logs, radar, and ADS-B data.

A NASA memorandum and mission operation report describe planned additional flight activities for the Gemini 4 (GT-4) mission, including extravehicular activity, a hand-held maneuvering unit, and a rendezvous demonstration with the spent booster second stage, which had flashing lights installed to aid the test.

A 1956 CIA information report from Hungary relays a November 1955 letter from a Budapest resident to a naturalized US citizen, describing fast-moving flying objects nicknamed 'flying saucers' that reportedly caused public unease and prompted scientific attention, with a claimed speed of 12,000 km/h. The letter included a sketch showing a formation of circular objects along a flight path between Budapest and Moscow.

An illustration depicts two silhouetted figures viewing a line of small red lights alongside a larger bright orb in a night sky over a desert landscape. No accompanying document text specifies who observed the lights or when.

An AARO memorandum presents a first-hand narrative from a federal law enforcement agent describing glowing orbs and lights observed over two nights in October 2023 in the western United States, including a hovering vehicle-like object and a translucent floating object seen while conducting field work.

A billeting officer at Muroc Air Field, California reported seeing two silver, spherical or disc-shaped objects flying about 300 MPH at roughly 8000 feet, headed north; other personnel corroborated the sighting, and a third similar object was later seen circling tightly over the airfield.

A December 1953 CIA memo reviews the status of the UFOB project, describing US Air Force, Navy, and Army handling of unidentified flying object reports, an Air Force camera program to photograph such objects at radar sites, and brief mentions of foreign government interest including Canada, Great Britain, and Sweden.

A CIA information report transmits a translated article by German scientist Dr. Eduard Ludwig, submitted for publication in a Chilean German-language magazine, offering a theoretical aerodynamic explanation for reported 'flying discs' based on rotary gas-turbine and boundary-layer/Flettner-rotor concepts from wartime German aircraft research.

The image shows a nighttime rendering of two occupants inside a vehicle observing a bright, round light hovering above a road ahead. No document text accompanies the image to provide details of the reported event.

A rendered image depicts two silhouetted figures observing a glowing orb-like light in the night sky above a desert mountain landscape. No accompanying document text is present to establish details of an incident.

This is a cover and title page for Project Blue Book Special Report No. 14, dated 5 May 1955, described as an analysis of reports of unidentified aerial objects compiled by the Air Technical Intelligence Center at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Ohio. The document itself contains no specific incident narrative on these pages.
An image shows two silhouetted figures standing on a hillside at night, facing away from the camera and looking toward a bright light or celestial object in the sky.

An FBI report describes a site survey conducted at a private residence in November 2024 to review areas where the property owner reported observing unidentified aerial phenomena over the past three years, including a treeline and trail camera locations. Agents were shown photos and video from trail cameras and the property owner offered to allow additional equipment to be installed for future monitoring.

This is a NASA routing slip and attached draft debriefing notes for Gemini IX (1966), covering micrometeorite collection experiments and astronaut observations, including a note that Stafford reported seeing a bright meteor while looking down at Earth. The document does not describe any unidentified aerial phenomenon.

A 1952 CIA memorandum reports on British activity regarding unidentified flying objects, describing a standing UK committee on flying saucers and an incident at a Yorkshire RAF field about ten to twelve days prior in which senior RAF officials and pilots reportedly observed a 'perfect flying saucer' during a demonstration, generating public press coverage. The memo also references a 1946 Swedish incident and a Naval film from the Tremonton incident, and a follow-up page discusses general potential dangers such as identification delays, mass hysteria, and communications overload from such reports.

This is the cover and preface material for a Gemini V (GT-5) technical flight crew debriefing transcript, Part II, prepared by NASA's Spacecraft Operations Branch and covering a debriefing session held August 30 through September 2, 1965 at Cape Kennedy, Florida. The document describes crew comments on mission operations rather than any anomalous aerial phenomenon.

These are official transmittal letters from 1953 concerning a report by a panel of scientific consultants on the subject of unidentified flying objects, convened by the CIA at the recommendation of the Intelligence Advisory Committee, and forwarded to civil defense and national security officials for review.

An Australian Department of Defence Joint Intelligence Organization minute paper discusses attached reports on scientific and intelligence aspects of unidentified aerial reports, referencing US Air Force, CIA, and Project Blue Book records, plus a database of alleged weapon-system-related sightings compiled by researchers at Northwestern University. The accompanying summary alleges that US intelligence agencies used public debunking efforts as a cover for domestic development of advanced propulsion technology.

A pastor from McMinnville, Oregon wrote to FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover describing an observation from the previous May of four narrow beams of light converging over the Cascade Mountains, with small clouds forming within the beams and an apparent explosion effect at their meeting point rising to about ten thousand feet, visible for at least ten minutes. Hoover's office replied thanking him for the information without further comment.

An FBI electronic communication describes special agents conducting a site survey around a redacted property on 12/2024, walking the perimeter and checking areas where UAP activity had reportedly been observed on 11/2024. Agents found no unusual objects at ground or treetop level and assessed the tree cover made nighttime drone or craft flight in the area unrealistic.

A federal law enforcement agent's field narrative describes a two-day October 2023 observation in a western U.S. wilderness area involving a large orange/red hovering light, smaller red orbs released from it, and later a red-and-white light pair and faint formation lights near a roadway, some viewed with night vision goggles and thermal binoculars.

An image depicts two silhouetted figures beside a car observing a curved line of small red lights and two pairs of white lights in the night sky above a mountain range; no accompanying document text is present to establish witness details or circumstances.

An image shows two silhouetted figures at night looking toward a bright point of light in the sky above a desert landscape. No accompanying text or documentation is present to describe the circumstances of the observation.

This is a 1949 Department of the Army Plans & Operations Division file cover/routing sheet for an evaluation study of a phenomenon referred to as 'flying saucers,' with no specific incident details provided.

An FBI FD-302 interview report describes two witnesses at a private residence who observed a bright red spherical object with a white glowing center hovering near their backyard tree line, later joined by an identical second orb; the two orbs maneuvered together before appearing to merge and depart. Weeks later, one witness reported seeing several smaller white orbs passing overhead at higher altitude.

A CIA intelligence information report on the Sary Shagan weapons testing range in the USSR includes a field account from a former Soviet citizen who reported observing, one evening in late summer 1973, a bright green circular object or mass in the sky near Site 7 that widened and formed concentric green circles before its coloring disappeared within minutes, with no associated sound.

An FBI FD-302 report documents a witness interview describing multiple sightings: a yellow ball of light and a larger second orb seen near trees behind the witness's home, which appeared to merge before disappearing, followed weeks later by several white orbs traveling overhead at higher altitude. The witness also recalled a separate 1987 incident near power lines involving a red light that circled and hovered before landing.

A document describes five U.S. Army service members at Fort Carson, Colorado, who reported observing a stationary, angular, potato-shaped airborne object above and behind Cheyenne Mountain for roughly 30-180 seconds before it disappeared. The analyst assessed with low confidence that the object may have been sunlight backscattering off snow-covered terrain and low clouds, noting no anomalous data or unknown adversarial capability.

An FBI electronic communication documents an October 2024 interview with a redacted individual who reported observing lights near their property beginning in November 2021, including one instance where a single light split into two or three lights forming a triangular pattern for about ten minutes. The interviewee also described a 1996 incident where a vehicle's electronics reportedly shut down after being illuminated by a large light, and stated they used ADS-B flight data to try to rule out aircraft as the source of the sightings.

locationRaw: Chang La, Fukche, and Koyul, Ladakh, India
summary: A CIA information report describes multiple sightings of unidentified flying objects over Ladakh, Nepal, Sikkim, and Bhutan between February and March 1968. One entry recounts a 4 March 1968 sighting near Chang La, Fukche, and Koyul in Ladakh in which observers reported a white light traveling east to west accompanied by two blasting sounds, followed by a reddish light and white smoke.

This is a NASA preliminary transcript cover and preface for the Gemini IV flight crew debriefing, conducted aboard the recovery ship USS Wasp, covering operational aspects of the mission rather than any anomalous observation.

The document appears to be a heavily degraded scanned form or memo with multiple text blocks, but the poor image quality prevents reliable reading of its content or description of any reported aerial object.

An AARO memorandum records the first-hand narrative of Witness 4, a federal law enforcement agent, describing multiple sightings over two days in October 2023 in the western United States, plus later follow-up sightings. The witness reported orange/yellow/white orbs, clusters and lines of lights, lights that produced smaller lights, chemtrail-like phenomena, and a darker hovering object with a faint red light, all observed from ground vehicles at various distances and altitudes.
The image shows a bright orange spherical object near a mountain ridge above a desert road at night, with no accompanying documentation of witnesses, date, or location.
An image depicts a saucer-shaped object composited over a mountain landscape near a residential area, with no accompanying textual report of an observation.

This is a NASA technical debriefing document for the Gemini VII mission, containing a preface explaining the transcript was made from voice tape recordings of the flight crew debriefing conducted December 19-21, 1965 at Cape Kennedy, Florida. The document itself does not describe any aerial phenomenon or unidentified object sighting.

locationRaw: Between Baku and Tiflis, Azerbaijan SSR, USSR
summary: A CIA information report recounts a US business executive's account of observing a triangular object with three lights near an airfield along the Caspian Sea coast while traveling by train from Baku to Tiflis. The object reportedly performed several rapid spirals before climbing steeply at about 45 degrees, tracked by a searchlight, and a train steward later closed the compartment blinds reportedly on orders from an accompanying official.

An FBI electronic communication describes two special agents visiting a witness's home in November 2024 to confirm previously reported UAP sightings. During surveillance, agents observed pulsating white lights near a tree line that moved erratically, appeared and disappeared over roughly ten seconds, and did not appear to reflect off nearby water or illuminate the ground; five photographs taken were reported to be mostly blurry.

An FBI report describes a forensic sketch interview with a former U.S. Army intelligence officer who, along with four colleagues, observed a potato-shaped, opalescent object hovering over the Cheyenne Mountains for about two minutes before it reportedly vanished instantly without leaving a shadow.

A 1948 Navy directive from the Chief of Naval Operations, forwarded by the Commandant of the Fifth Naval District, instructs naval stations to report sightings of 'flying discs' or other unidentified aerial objects to the nearest Air Force command and Naval District Intelligence Office, and to obtain photographic evidence when possible.

This is a preliminary NASA transcript cover and preface for the Gemini V flight crew technical debriefing, describing the crew's general operational account of the mission recorded at Cape Kennedy, Florida in August-September 1965; it references a later section covering visual sightings among other topics but no sighting content is included in this excerpt.
